title: Understanding Why Your Cat is Staring Blankly at Walls
meta_desc: Learn why cats stare blankly at walls, when it becomes a medium-urgency concern, and how visual evidence helps your veterinarian with a diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Persistent blank staring in cats can indicate neurological issues or cognitive decline and usually warrants a veterinary visit within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if the behavior is accompanied by seizures, head pressing, or sudden blindness.

What does it mean if my cat is staring blankly at the wall or into space?

When a cat stares blankly at a wall or into space for extended periods, it is often more than just feline curiosity. In a clinical context, this behavior can be a sign of sensory changes, neurological issues, or cognitive decline. While cats have superior hearing and sight and might occasionally track a small insect or a sound behind the drywall, persistent and unresponsive staring is a clinical sign that needs to be addressed.

Is it an emergency if my cat is staring blankly?

  • Schedule a veterinary consultation within 24 to 48 hours for persistent blank staring.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the staring is accompanied by head pressing, seizures, or sudden blindness.
  • Monitor for a total lack of appetite, which may indicate an acute neurological or metabolic event.

Why should I record a video of my cat staring blankly for my veterinarian?

  • Capture a video at home to document symptoms that your cat may hide due to stress at the veterinary clinic.
  • Allow your veterinarian to observe the exact nature and duration of the staring episode.
  • Provide visual evidence of your cat’s pupil response or any subtle muscle tremors to aid in a neurological assessment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hallucinatory and locomotory compulsive disorders, which may manifest as staring blankly at walls, are less common in cats than in dogs. However, before considering a behavioral diagnosis, it is crucial to exclude underlying medical conditions, particularly painful conditions or any disease process affecting the neurologic system. Modifications to the environment that provide more control and predictability, along with increased enrichment, may be beneficial. In some cases, medications that augment brain serotonin, such as fluoxetine and clomipramine, may be considered.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Could my cat be suffering from feline dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, in senior cats, staring blankly is a common symptom of Feline Cognitive Dysfunction (FCD), which is similar to dementia in humans.

faqs.question: What is the difference between staring and 'head pressing'?
faqs.answer: Staring involves looking into space, whereas head pressing is the act of physically pushing the head against a wall or corner. Head pressing is a much more urgent medical emergency.

faqs.question: Can high blood pressure cause this behavior?
faqs.answer: Yes, hypertension is a common cause of staring or disorientation in cats, often related to underlying kidney or thyroid issues.
